cue: Tell someone to stop lamenting what's already done

es.meaning: What's done is done — square your shoulders and face it.
es.example: Ya firmaste, así que a lo hecho, pecho.
es.exampleTranslation: You've signed it, so face the music.

it.meaning: To lament what can no longer be changed (usually in the negative: don't).
it.example: Inutile piangere sul latte versato: pensiamo a domani.
it.exampleTranslation: No use crying over spilled milk — let's think about tomorrow.

bridge.note: Same predicament, opposite grammar of advice. Italian names the useless behaviour and tells you to stop it — don't cry over the milk. Spanish names the required posture instead and tells you to adopt it: chest out to what's done. One proverb forbids, the other prescribes; the Spanish is four syllables and a rhyme, the Italian a full clause.

interference.es_to_it: There is no milk in the Spanish idiom; 'llorar sobre la leche derramada' is a calque from English that natives will understand and not use.
interference.it_to_es: 'A lo hecho, pecho' has no Italian equivalent as compressed — the nearest is a full sentence, not a motto.

review.notes: From Joe's Italian proverb list 2026-07-18 (learner-site discovery; all glosses and examples authored fresh). ES counterpart chosen by Claude from recall — UNVERIFIED. Negative-vs-positive framing of the same advice. (Re-numbered from 0060 on 2026-07-18: Claude had assigned 0060 without checking and OVERWROTE the existing 'hacer puente / fare il ponte' entry. Restored from GitHub.)
